reasoning:
Response A is stronger across all criteria. Both responses choose a sensible right-sized stack (Go + Postgres + WebSockets + Docker Compose), but A is substantially more complete, precise, and security-aware.

On encryption, A gives a clearer end-to-end design: X3DH + Double Ratchet for 1:1, Sender Keys for groups, explicit forward secrecy behavior, membership-change rekeying, server-stored ciphertext structure, and concrete key restore options without server escrow. B is directionally correct but has more gaps/inconsistencies (e.g., less precise group rekey/removal handling, weaker detail on what exactly is stored and how key material is managed).

On invite design, A clearly models full lifecycle and abuse controls with better privacy properties (hash-based invite secret, URL fragment so token is not sent to server before redemption, signed invite records, revocation, replay/single-use controls, immutable audit chain). B’s token-link flow is simpler but less privacy-preserving and less rigorous on chain integrity/cascade semantics.

On threat model/security boundaries, A explicitly addresses malicious server observability limits, active attacks (key substitution MITM) and mitigations (safety numbers), DB exfiltration, device compromise blast radius, and invite-system limits. B covers the basics but is less nuanced and misses/understates some active attack vectors.

On self-hosting practicality, A provides a more operationally realistic runbook: concrete Compose layout, reverse proxy/TLS, backups (DB/media/config), offsite sync, health checks, metrics, and alerting. B is deployable but thinner operationally.

On depth/precision, A clearly meets all 7 required sections with detailed schema annotations (plaintext vs encrypted), real-time protocol behavior, explicit trade-offs, and implementation-level specifics. B is competent but noticeably more high-level.

Overall, A is the better system design response.
